# FDR {#sec-FDR}

**Objectives**

- Address the multiple testing issue

```{r load-pkg-fdr, include=FALSE}
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidyverse)
  library(patchwork)
  library(ExploreModelMatrix)
  library(SummarizedExperiment)
})
```

## Multiple testing

In the few examples of linear model that we have seen in the previous
section, the `lm()` function was always applied to a single gene. In a
real RNA-seq experiment, we will have a few thousand tests to perform,
as each gene is going to be tested.


```{r include=FALSE}
set.seed(2025)
exp <- matrix(rnorm(20000*6, mean = 100, sd = 20), ncol = 6)
rownames(exp) <- paste0("Gene", 1:20000)
colnames(exp) <- c(paste0("A", 1:3), paste0("B", 1:3))
exp <- log(exp)
exp <- as_tibble(exp, rownames = "Gene")
save(exp, file ="data/exp.rda")
```

Let's use the `exp` dataset that provides log expression values for 20,000 genes
in 6 samples, three in groupA and three in groupB.

```{r}
if (!file.exists("data/exp.rda")) {
    dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
    download.file(
        url = "https://github.com/UCLouvain-BIOINFO/bioinfo-training-03-rnaseq/data/exp.rda?raw=true",
        destfile = "data/exp.rda"
    )
}
load("data/exp.rda")
exp
```


Let's apply a t.test on all these genes:

```{r}
res <- exp %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(pval = t.test(c(A1, A2, A3), c(B1,B2,B3))$p.value) %>%
  ungroup() # to revert the rowwise()
res
```

#### Question {-}

How many significant genes did we obtained ? Which genes are of possible
biological interest?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```{r}
table(res$pval < 0.05)
res %>% filter(pval < 0.05) %>%
  arrange(pval)
```

</details>

The data above have been generated with the `rnorm()` function for all samples.

This is the code that generated the data:

```{r, eval = F}
set.seed(2025)
exp <- matrix(rnorm(20000*6, mean = 100, sd = 20), ncol = 6)
rownames(exp) <- paste0("Gene", 1:20000)
colnames(exp) <- c(paste0("A", 1:3), paste0("B", 1:3))
exp <- log(exp)
exp <- as_tibble(exp, rownames = "Gene")
```

#### Question {-}

- Do you still think any of the features show significant differences?

- Why do we obtain genes with a p-value < 0.05?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

A pvalue of 0.05 means that there is only 5% chance of getting this data if no
real difference existed (if the null hypothesis was really true).

In other words, choosing a cut off of 0.05 means there is 5% chance that the
wrong decision is made (resulting in a false positive).

```{r echo=FALSE}
sign_genes <- res %>% filter(pval < 0.05)
unsign_genes <- res %>% filter(pval > 0.05)

res_long <- res %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "sample", values_to = "expression", -c(Gene, pval)) %>%
  mutate(group = substr(sample, 1, 1))

fig1 <- res_long %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = expression, fill = group, color = group)) +
  geom_density(alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(data = res_long %>% filter(Gene == unsign_genes$Gene[1]),
             aes(x = expression, y = 0, color = group), size = 3) +
  ggtitle(paste0(unsign_genes$Gene[1], " (pvalue = ", round(unsign_genes$pval[1], 5), ")"))

fig2 <- res_long %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = expression, fill = group, color = group)) +
  geom_density(alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(data = res_long %>% filter(Gene == sign_genes$Gene[1]),
             aes(x = expression, y = 0, color = group), size = 3) +
  ggtitle(paste0(sign_genes$Gene[1], " (pvalue = ", round(sign_genes$pval[1], 5), ")"))

fig1 / fig2
```

</details>

#### Question {-}

If you were to visualize the p-value distribution using a histogram,
how would you expect it to look like?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```{r, fig.height=4, fig.width=7, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
res %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pval)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, color = "black", fill = "gray")
```

</details>


```{r eval=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Do jelly beans cause acne? Scientists investigate. From [xkcd](https://xkcd.com/882/).", include=FALSE, purl=FALSE, results='markup'}
#[This xkcd cartoon](https://xkcd.com/882/) humorously illustrates the multiple
#testing issue by depicting scientists testing whether eating jelly beans causes acne.

knitr::include_graphics("./fig/jellybeans.png")
```

Now, let's slightly modify our simulated expression data.

In a typical RNA-seq experiment, we may test around 20,000 genes, but
only a fraction of them are expected to be truly differentially
expressed. We could imagine a drug treatment that would modify the
expression of about 1000 genes, but that would have no impact on the
other ones.

To mimic this, we are going to simulate a strong up-regulation for the
first 1000 genes by increasing their expression values in group B.

```{r, fig.height=4, fig.width=8, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
exp_modified <- exp

exp_modified[1:1000, c("B1", "B2", "B3")] <- log(matrix(rnorm(1000*3, mean = 300, sd = 20), ncol = 3))

exp_modified <- exp_modified %>%
  mutate(DE = c(rep(TRUE, 1000), rep(FALSE, 19000)))

exp_modified
```

Let's run a t-test on this new dataset:

```{r}
res_modified <- exp_modified %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(pval = t.test(c(A1, A2, A3), c(B1, B2, B3), var = TRUE)$p.value) %>%
  ungroup()
table(res_modified$pval < 0.05)
```


Now let's look at p-values histogram of the first 1000 genes (for
which Ho is FALSE), and of the remaining genes.


```{r echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=15, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
h1 <- res_modified %>% filter(DE) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pval)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, color = "black", fill = "gray") +
  xlim(0, 1) +
  ylim(0, 1500) +
  ggtitle("Ho is FALSE")+
  theme_bw() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1,
        panel.grid = element_blank())

h2 <- res_modified %>% filter(!DE) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pval)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, color = "black", fill = "gray") +
  xlim(0, 1) +
  ylim(0, 1500) +
  ggtitle("Ho is TRUE") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1,
        panel.grid = element_blank())

h3 <- res_modified %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pval)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, boundary = 0, color = "black", fill = "gray") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 950, color = "red", lwd = 1) +
  ggtitle("All genes") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 1,
        panel.grid = element_blank())

h1 + h2 + h3
```

The figure on the right illustrates the principle behind the FDR adjustment.
The False discovery rate (FDR) is the expected proportion of false positives
among all the results considered as "significant" (i.e. pval < 0.05).
The adjustment procedure estimate the proportion of hypothesis that are null
and then adjust the p-values accordingly.


## Adjustment for multiple testing

The False discovery rate (FDR) computes the expected fraction of false
positives among all discoveries. It allows us to choose n results with a given FDR.
Widely used examples are Benjamini-Hochberg or q-values.

let's use the `p.adjust()` function to adjust the pvalues.

```{r}
res_modified <- res_modified %>%
  mutate(padj = p.adjust(pval, method = "BH"))

table(sign_padj = res_modified$padj < 0.05, sign_pval = res_modified$pval < 0.05)
```

Only `r table(res_modified$padj < 0.05)["TRUE"]` were found to be significant after FDR correction.


#### Question {-}

- How many genes would you expect to remain significant after FDR
  correction in the first dataset (`res` table) ?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```{r}
res <- res %>%
  mutate(padj = p.adjust(pval, method = "BH"))
table(res$pval < 0.05)
table(res$padj < 0.05)
```

</details>
